sentences.text: [TONE]
So we are now end of November, beginning of December 1945.
And although everything is wonderful, there is this great sadness that my brother has not survived, contrary to our expectations, perhaps naive.
But throughout the war in Poland, we took for granted that anybody who has escaped from the German occupation has every chance of being alive and surviving the war.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that wasn't to be.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] George, similar to my father and myself, felt he wanted to do his bit.

And I think, in a way, the fact that mother and I were in Poland under the Germans made him focus more on the fight against the Germans, although originally he joined the Royal Air Force and was flying in Burma against the Japanese.
At the end of '43, he got himself transferred to the Polish Air Force, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saying that his fight was against the Germans.
That's who he wanted to fight.
That's where he had something to fight against.
So that was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his objective.
And he was very brave, because we heard afterwards he was decorated, posthumously, with a cross of valour.

And when my parents went to the general command of the Polish Air Force, he told them that George had already seen him before his-- his flight, knowing that he had only one more flight to do of the second round and had volunteered for path-finding duties.
So his chances of survival over the next 10 months until the war ended were probably very, very small.
In any case, that was a great tragedy for both my mother and myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have a memory of an 18-year-old brother, whom I never saw as a man.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Once we were in Italy, there was a question of, what was I going to do?
There was-- I needed to go to school.
There was a school for families in the south of Italy.
But having had me for such a short time, Father really didn't want me to go away.
So somehow, he arranged that by falsifying my date of birth and my age, I could join the army.
The problem was that, although the powers to be knew that I was younger, nobody told the medical commission that I was 15 and not 18.
The result was that I got category-- medical category C, and was rejected as being underdeveloped-- [LAUGHS] for age 18.
So subsequently, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was granted special permission by General Anders, the commander of the Polish Second Corps, to be accepted into the army despite my category C.
And thereby, I was able to go to a secondary school for eight years, which was located only a short distance from where my father was.
It was located in Porto San Giorgio.
And I met there a number of my friends from the Girl Guides in Warsaw, who knew when I was younger, but that didn't matter.
And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I must say that during a period, '39-'45, my education was sketchy, to say the least.
I had periods when I had some lessons.
I had periods when I didn't have any.
I learned a little bit of Latin.
I learned a little bit of maths, history because I was reading books.
I was quite well read in literature.
But I had never done any physics or chemistry or biology.
So I had a very strange education.
I don't think I spent more than a year in school in five years.
But when I went to the school at Porto San Giorgio, I went into the top class before O level and somehow managed it.
So I got my equivalent-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH], lower matriculation in Polish.
During the Easter holidays, I was sent to a recuperation center on Lake Como, because I wasn't very strong at that time.
And in August '45, the whole school and some of the other units left Italy as part of army repatriation.
And we came to England, landed at Liverpool, traveled on the a splendid ship, arrived in Liverpool at the end of August-- rain, cold, miserable.
We went to a camp in West Kirby, from where we were transferred to another camp near Hereford at Foxley.
And my parents were still in Italy at that time.
But my father's brother, the journalist, was a member of the editorial team of the Polish newspaper in England, That he and his wife and son, who was born in 1944, were living in London.
So I came to see them.
And very soon, I decided that I really wanted to go to an English school.
If we were going to stay in England, there was no point in staying in the Polish school.
So when my parents arrived in England in October, I put it to them that I thought it was time I moved on.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother's brother, with his wife and son, who before the war lived in Belgium, were also in London.
And the son, who was a year younger than I am, was at Latymer Upper School in London.
So he was consulted on girls' schools and suggested that perhaps I could go to Godolphin and Latymer, which is a sister school of Latymer Upper School.
And my father went to see the headmistress, Ms.
Joyce Bishop, who was an absolutely remarkable person, who told my father that, in fact, she had one girl who had come to Godolphin the previous year from a concentration camp.
Her name was Anna Ambramska.
Yes.
Who did her school certificate in one year.
And her comment was, if Polish children are so determined, we have to help them.
And she accepted me.
So I went out of uniform and into a tunic in December 1945-- no, '46, sorry-- '46-- into the upper fifths to prepare for my school certificate.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Although I spoke some English, it wasn't great.
And some of the subjects were completely beyond me.
I mean, you try having a double period of physics or chemistry, where your English is shaky and you had never done it before.
Then you have maths, with purchase and yards, when I've been brought up on decimal system.
And said books-- Macbeth-- By Easter, I did my marks, and I did badly.
So Ms.
Bishop called my father and said, well, we don't like our girls to fail.
I suggest that Barbara take another year.
She's only had a few months in school.
Father came home and told me his verdict.
And I said, no way.
Either I'm doing my school cert in the summer or I'm leaving school.
My father was very understanding.
He went back and said, I have to take her views into account.
She's looked up to herself for so long.
If she says she's going to do it, she's going to do it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the school were wonderful.
Throughout the Easter holidays, a retired headmistress, friend of Ms.
Bishop, took me for English every day.
Ms.
Bishop herself took me between lessons for English literature.
All the other teachers were helping me.
And I took my school certificate in the summer.
I could probably show you the note-- my result note from Ms.
Bishop.
I passed.
I got distinction in Polish, which, it may sound easy, but it wasn't.
Because I had to translate into English a thirteenth-century Polish hymn.
My English wasn't up to it.
My Polish was fine.
But it was the English, of course-- absolute hell, trying to think of the right words.
But I got distinction in that.
I got a credit in French.
I passed-- I got a credit in English.
I passed in English literature.
I passed in maths.
I think I passed in geography.
I failed physics and biology, but I got exemption from matriculation.
And by that time, we were talking about what I was going to do.
My father really wanted me to do something which is international, like pharmacy.
But without chemistry and so on, that wasn't an option.
And I wanted to do something with languages.
So we compromised that I would try and do a B-com degree, which included a foreign language.
It was going to be French.
Ms.
Bishop was most surprised, and said she'd never heard of a girl doing it.
And it involved doing a group four higher school certificate, which they were not doing at Godolphins.
But she arranged for me to go to [INAUDIBLE] grammar school, where there was a boys' school and a girls' school in the same building-- separate, but in the same building.
And I must have been one of the early girls in a boys' school, because I had all my lessons with the boys.
And I decided that I wanted to go to LSE.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in January '49, I took what was known as the Leverhulme scholarship examination, which was an entrance for all London university colleges.
And my first choice was LSE.
My second choice was University College.
And that consisted of an essay paper and a general knowledge paper.
And imagine my surprise when I opened the examination paper for the essays, and the first essay subject was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my God.

[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Something's a dreadful curse-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] foreign rule is a dreadful curse, and the minor benefits it can confer can never compensate for the spiritual degradation it involves.
Well, I think the examiner was looking for an essay on colonialism.
It did not read like that to me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wrote for three hours.
And I never read it-- essay.
I don't know what the English was like.
In any case, it got me a place both at University College and at LSE.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I started at LSE in October 1949, just after my 19th birthday, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] having had a strange education, to say the least.
But I hadn't lost any time.
That's a great achievement.
Didn't stop me.
Very well.
All the girls at school took a great interest in me, were very helpful, very friendly.
I was-- I mean, even when I-- when I was in Polish school at Foxley, we made contact with the local Girl Guides, and we were invited out.
No, I must say, I was made very, very welcome.
Well, they-- my father was army, in the Polish Army-- it was called the Polish Resettlement Corps in England-- through '47.
They were in a-- a camp near Amersham-- Hodgemoor Camp.
And then they came to London.
He found a half-brother of my grandfather's here, who had come to England from Germany, who was a director of a company here.
And he gave him his first job.
But we lived in West Kensington with-- in one room and kitchenette.
I slept in the kitchenette on a camp bed and my parents in the other room.
So the first summer, my father had been in England after the collapse of France.
He was stationed near Ipswich.
And he made quite a number of friends, English friends, in the area.
Yes.
Well, my father spoke English before the war because he was--
Well, she-- she learned it gradually.

But because he had English friends, the first summer I was in England, '47-- summer of '47, I was invited to stay with a very nice family near Ipswich-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hans, I think, who had daughters of my age.
But although it was-- they were terribly kind and terribly nice, they really couldn't quite understand the sort of experiences I had been through.
And on one occasion, I remember my contemporary was invited to some ball.
And she said, well, why don't you ring up your mother and ask her to send you your dress?
Well, I had no-- I didn't have a dress.
I hadn't ever been to a ball.
It was a different world for me.
Did you ever talk to any of your friends about the experiences you'd had during the war?
A little, but not about the ghetto at that time.
I did about the Warsaw rising.
In fact, I-- I gave talks to a number of schools.
I was asked to go to some Poles and tell them about the Warsaw rising.
No.
No.
I don't know.
It just-- it didn't come up.
At-- at that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think one didn't talk about it.
One of my grandmother's brothers lived in Israel.
And he was my sort of favorite great uncle.
I remember him coming to London and taking me out to tea at Lady Marx, who had a penthouse at Dorchester.
I had my first banana there since before the war.
And then she sent me tickets for a pantomime.
Well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was so strange going to an English pantomime.
I couldn't understand what it was all about-- didn't fit in.
But I can't even remember her asking me anything about the war.
Yeah, but I didn't think anybody was particularly interested at that time.
Yes.
Oh, yeah.
Then they came-- then they were-- they were released and they came back.
And we have kept with them.
Mrs.
Nowodworska died of cancer, breast cancer, quite a few years ago.
But when my parents were more established, she used to come and stay with my parents.
And--
Oh yes.
Yes.
Yes, Mrs.
Wedecka's daughter was in England.
And Mrs.
Wedecka came here and lived here until she died.
So, again, we've been friends with that family.
And Mrs.
Nowodworska's daughter, my contemporary, is still a great friend.
And we've-- in fact, we spent our last holiday in Poland with them, just before my husband died.
I don't think it really-- I don't even think that they asked those questions.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think it mattered.
It was people-- person to person things.
They were doing it for us as people.
Yes.
Or they got to know us.
Mrs.
Nowodworska never knew us before the war.
They just took a liking to us.
I don't know.
Yes, yes.
Definitely.
There was nothing in it for them.
I completed my degree in 1944-- sorry, '54-- no, '53-- '53, sorry, by which time I had met my husband-to-be.
We got engaged just after I got my degree.
Yes.
My husband was Polish.
He-- his father was a colonel in the Polish army, army family.
He took active part in the resistance and escaped from Poland in '45 when the communists took over, went to Italy.
His father was a prisoner of war during the war.
He was a senior officer at Murnau prisoners-of-war camp.
And he came to England, same as we did.
As it happened, my father became a friend of his cousin and her husband in Italy.
They were also in prisoner-of-war camps after the Warsaw rising.
They came to Italy.
And they were my father's partners in bridge.
In any--
Well, that-- that's why I bring them in, because the lady was a dentist.
And when she came to England, she set up a dental practice.
And of course, all our family were her patients.
My husband, the young cousin, was living with her.
So I met him in the dentist's surgery.
[LAUGHS] And he was-- he went to university.
He was studying engineering.
And he graduated in '54.
And we got married that summer.
One of the requests of my father when we wanted to get engaged was that he should write and tell his parents of my background.
The fact that our family were originally Jewish.
He was Roman Catholic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father said he didn't want any surprises and he wanted full approval, which was granted without any questions.
His parents, both together and separately, came to England many times and became great friends of my parents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was married for just over 40 years before my husband died.
And in good times and bad times, this was something which was never brought up, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which I think is greatly to his credit.
Yes, but the same applied to his family.
My mother-- my mother had one cousin in Warsaw, Zosia Numberg.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my husband's family became great friends and supporters.
Up to the time of her death, kept in regular contact with her.
When-- first time I went to Poland after the war in 1971--
With my husband, to visit his parents, we stayed in Warsaw.
Strange.
Found it very odd to be regarded as a tourist somewhere which, to me, was home.
But then everything was very different.
But we had a great welcome from Mrs.
Nowodworska and all her family, a wonderful family luncheon for us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we went to the Jewish cemetery.
My elder daughter at that time would have been 14, the younger one, 9.
We hadn't really spoken before of the Jewish origins of the family.
So there was-- we said we're taking them to see the graves of the great-grandparents.
And they saw the wall, a Jewish cemetery, which consisted of fragments of those monuments, which were destroyed by the Germans.
So it's a big wall.
And, of course, it's got Hebrew writing on it.
And they said, what is it?
And it was my husband who told them that this is where my family are buried because they were of Jewish-- they were Jewish.
There was never any question.
There was never any problem with it.
They accepted--
Absolutely.
It never mattered.
They know about their family.
They don't-- we don't talk of the Jewish background-- the Jewish background.
We talk of the family background.
The people.
And the experiences.
So they know about to ghetto.
But it's in terms of family.
They know I am very proud of the number of generations of my family which have gone to university-- my grandfather, my father, myself, my daughter, both my daughters.
And I say to my grandchildren, you are the fifth generation which is going to be university-educated, hopefully.
That's what matters.
